index.title: nautli | Cross-AI memory on your disk
index.description: Local-first memory shared by Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, and other MCP clients. AI asks. You decide.

manifesto.title: Manifesto | nautli
manifesto.description: Why your AI memory should live on your disk, keep its history, and wait for your decisions.

install.title: Install | nautli

faq.title: FAQ | nautli
faq.description: Clear answers about data transmission, Claude judgments, Obsidian safety, removal, pricing, and Supporters.

home.h1: 95% fewer memory tokens. No hallucinations from conflicting notes.
home.subline: Private memory on your disk, shared across Claude, Codex, and Cursor, organized every night.
home.trustLine: Your notes never reach our servers · No silent rewrites · MIT · macOS
home.boundaryTitle: What nautli reads, writes, and sends

boundaries.title: Reads
lines: Markdown notes in the selected scope
A checkup does not modify source notes

boundaries.title: Writes
lines: One marked instruction block when enabled
MCP and launchd settings only when enabled
Each integration has a removal path

boundaries.title: Sends
lines: Note content to nautli servers: none
Judgment prompts through your Claude subscription to Anthropic
Optional collection is off by default
Shared checkups contain numbers, not notes

trustCards.title: Your notes stay off our servers
trustCards.body: The local store, event logs, and reports remain under ~/.nautli. Check active connections while nautli runs.

trustCards.title: Files remain the source of truth
trustCards.body: Facts and their history stay in local files. The search index can be rebuilt from those files.

trustCards.title: Ambiguity becomes a question
trustCards.body: Contradictions are placed in a review queue. A decision becomes active only after you answer.

home.factIntro: A fact keeps the claim, its source, and the decision it replaced.
home.howTitle: The models can ask. Only you can settle the record.
home.howIntro: While you sleep, nautli surfaces conflicts as questions. Claude Code, Codex, and Cursor read the same approved memory. Nothing is rewritten until you decide.

after.speaker: nautli
after.text: PostgreSQL and SQLite conflict. Did the decision change?

after.speaker: Every connected AI
after.text: SQLite is current. PostgreSQL remains in history.

home.checkIntro: Run a checkup to see real contradictions, changed decisions, and duplicates before importing anything.
home.emptyState: Run nautli checkup to fill this card.

reviews.title: Two current ports
reviews.body: One note says the API uses port 3000. Another current note says port 4000.
reviews.note: nautli asks which claim is current. It applies neither on its own.

reviews.title: PostgreSQL became SQLite
reviews.body: The later note explicitly replaces the earlier database decision.
reviews.note: This is history, not an error. The new fact supersedes the old one without deleting it.

reviews.title: The same retry rule twice
reviews.body: Two notes state the same retry limit with the same scope and meaning.
reviews.note: The review shows both sources before any consolidation.

compareRows: Memory may be sent to a hosted service
Notes do not reach nautli servers
home.supporterBody: Core software stays MIT licensed. Supporter names appear only with explicit opt-in.
home.discordBody: Use Discord for implementation questions and project discussion.
stats: a day is all it asks

rows: Delete the past to make the index tidy
Keep the store on your disk
Make a hosted account the source of truth
essay: AI models change every six months. Your decisions do not.
A model is a tool you may replace. The reasoning, constraints, and choices behind your work belong to you. They should survive a new model, a new editor, and a new subscription.
nautli gives connected AI clients one local record. Each fact keeps its source. When two claims disagree, nautli raises a question instead of quietly rewriting the past.
Your answer becomes the current fact. The older fact remains in history, so a changed decision stays legible rather than looking like an error.
Memory should be portable, inspectable, and governed by the person whose work it represents. That is the boundary we build around.

install.title: Start with an inspectable local store
install.intro: These steps create the store, then let you check a folder before choosing any additional integrations.
requirements: macOS for the automatic nightly launchd job
Installed and logged in for duplicate and contradiction judgments
Required for the memory checkup step
Core CLI and MCP server run where Node.js runs; schedule daemon-run yourself
changes: init creates the local store, config, index, and reports here
Back up anything you need, then remove this directory
MCP registration is added only when you enable Claude Code integration
One marked nautli instruction block is added only when enabled
The nightly macOS job is added only when enabled
nautli provides a snippet; you paste it manually
Remove the nautli entry you added
install.undoNote: The three commands above create only ~/.nautli. Integrations and the nightly job require separate opt-in setup steps.
install.testBody: Use an empty folder to inspect the result shape before pointing nautli at personal or work notes.
install.helpBody: Run nautli doctor, then share its non-sensitive result in GitHub Issues or ask in Discord.

faq.title: Questions worth asking before install
faq.intro: The short version: your store is local, judgments are explicit, and every integration can be removed.

items.question: Where do the 95% and coin-flip numbers come from?
items.answer: Measured on our founder's 606-note vault. Always-loaded instruction files cost about 22,000 tokens every session; a nautli recall averaged 570 tokens per question in our logs, so a typical session uses roughly 95% fewer memory tokens. The checkup found 49 pairs of notes that contradict each other; when both sides of a contradiction reach a model, the answer for that topic is effectively a coin flip. After you settle each review question, only the fact you chose is served. Your numbers will differ; run a checkup to see them.

items.question: What leaves my machine?
items.answer: Your notes do not go to nautli servers. Duplicate and contradiction judgment prompts are sent through your own Claude subscription to Anthropic. Optional collection is off by default.

items.question: What does the judge send to Claude?
items.answer: It sends the text needed to compare candidate facts for duplicate and contradiction judgments. It does not send your whole vault, and a failed or ambiguous judgment becomes no action or a review question.

items.question: Will a checkup change my Obsidian vault?
items.answer: No. A checkup reads Markdown notes in the folders you select and does not modify the source notes. You can exclude top-level folders before it starts. Importing findings writes to ~/.nautli, not back into the vault.

items.question: How do I remove nautli?
items.answer: Remove the marked instruction block with nautli setup --step remove-instructions, remove the launchd job with nautli setup --step uninstall-daemon, remove MCP registrations, then back up and delete ~/.nautli if you no longer need the local memory.

items.question: What does it cost, and what is a Supporter?
items.answer: The core is open source under the MIT License. Supporters fund maintenance. Public recognition is optional and off unless a supporter explicitly opts in.

share.emptyTitle: No result data in this link
share.emptyBody: A shared result appears only when the URL contains numeric checkup fields. No note text is accepted.
share.title: Memory checkup
share.resultNote: This card contains numbers only. Note content stays on its owner's machine.
share.turnBody: Run the checkup locally. You choose the folder and decide what to import.
